topic: Areas of Inquiry: Music Technology in the Electronic and Digital Age
subject: music

items.q: What three notes form a major triad in root position?
items.why: Students think 'three notes = a triad'. Triads are specific: stacked thirds from the root. C major = C, E, G (skipping D and F). Consecutive scale notes are clusters, not triads.

items.q: What is the function of the bridge in a pop song?
items.correct: To provide contrast before the final chorus, often by changing melody, key, or texture.
items.misconception: It is the same as the chorus.
items.random_wrong: It is the very first section.
items.why: Students confuse bridge with chorus because both are 'big' sections. The bridge appears once (usually after the second chorus) to provide CONTRAST and renew interest before returning.

items.q: What distinguishes Classical (1750-1820) from Baroque style?
items.correct: Clearer melodic phrases, homophonic texture, balanced structures like sonata form.
items.random_wrong: Classical means any old serious music.
items.why: Students use 'classical' loosely. The Classical period specifically reacted against Baroque complexity - simpler textures, clearer cadences, and balanced phrase structures (Mozart, Haydn, early Beethoven).
Correct answer: Leon Theremin. “Robert Moog” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Whose 1956 Gesang der Junglinge mixed boy's voice with electronics?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Stockhausen. “Berio” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was Ableton Live first released?
items.why: Exact answer: 2001 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
